ground_truth.title: The Programming Language Death Spiral - How Small Languages Die in the Age of AI
ground_truth.author: Hamilton Greene
ground_truth.main_content:
The programming language death spiral has been top of mind for me as I shift my primary language from F# (a top 50 lang) to C# (a top 5 lang). I first discussed this phenomenon back in 2023 in the State of F# as the greatest existential threat to the language and ecosystem.

Big Languages have a virtuous cycle: Lots of companies use mainstream languages, lots of jobs are available, lots of projects are built, lots of people learn them, and new companies are built with them. This creates a feedback loop where more companies mean more jobs/funding, more projects/resources, more people learning, and more companies built with them.

Small languages have a vicious cycle - a death spiral: Few companies use it meaning less jobs/funding, fewer projects/resources, fewer people learning it, and fewer companies built with it. When new tools come around, they can escape this spiral but must be much better than predecessors or have a killer feature.

AI amplifies these adoption cycles. AI coding proficiency is currently directly tied to training data. Top 10 langs have 10-100x more code examples and docs online. When comparing C# (3.329%) and F# (0.107%) by activity, we see a ~30x difference. AI companies will have higher monetary incentive to benchmark and improve in mainstream languages.

Small languages can make an comeback but it is an uphill battle. They need more public code examples, more blog posts, and more prodding AI companies to benchmark, tweak, and train for their languages. The onus is on the few users of small languages - no large company is going to do this for them.
with: The basic premise is: Lots of companies use mainstream languages, lots of jobs are available in them, lots of projects are built in them, lots of people learn them, new companies are built in these languages.

Small languages have a vicious cycle - a death spiral: Few companies use it meaning less jobs / funding, fewer jobs / funding available means less projects / resources, fewer projects / resources means fewer people learn it.

AI is a big shift in how we build software: Essentially replaced programming reference sites like Stack Overflow, can build small well-defined features reliably, slowly improving at building larger more complex features.
AI coding proficiency is currently directly tied to training data. Top 10 langs have 10-100x more code examples and docs online than those outside it.

When we compare AI coding today in large vs small langs, we can expect a 2-3x difference in code quality. This gap gets wider depending on the gap in usage and if a lang is small enough, it may not have critical mass of training data.
